title: Making a piped box cushion cover for a window seat
subtitle: Sew a fabric cover for a rectangular foam seat cushion with piped seams on the top panel and a zip-opening gusset
excerpt: How to make a box cushion cover with piping for a window seat. Covers cutting the top and base panels and the gusset strip, making and attaching the piping, sewing the panels to the gusset, and fitting the zip.
sourceNotes: UK sewing and upholstery trade practice for box cushion covers. Professional Upholstery Workshop, David James (referenced, not reproduced). Traditional UK soft-furnishing techniques.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cord of twisted cotton or polypropylene fibres used as the core of upholstery piping. Fabric is cut on the bias and folded around the cord, then stitched close to the cord with a piping or zipper foot. The covered cord is sewn into seams of cushion covers, chair backs, and loose covers to give a defined, tailored edge. No. 3 cord (4 mm) is standard for seating; No. 5 cord (6 mm) gives a bolder profile on larger pieces.

content.text: edges gives a window seat cushion a clean, tailored finish. The cover is a top panel, a base panel, and a continuous gusset strip (piping inserted at the seam where the top panel meets the gusset. A zip in the back gusset allows the foam to be removed for washing.

Measure the foam exactly: length, width, and depth. Add 15 mm seam allowance to all cut edges. The top panel is cut to the foam length plus 30 mm × width plus 30 mm. The base panel is the same. The gusset is cut in strips the depth of the foam plus 30 mm wide; join strips to make the full perimeter plus 100 mm overlap at the zip. Cut bias strips for the piping 40 mm wide from corner to corner of the fabric.

Fold the bias strip around the piping cord and stitch close to the cord with a zipper foot. Join bias strips on the diagonal for a flat seam that does not show through the piping. Baste the piping to the right-side perimeter of the top panel, raw edges aligned, clipping the bias at each corner so it curves without puckering. Overlap the ends neatly.
Sew the gusset to the top panel

Pin the gusset strip to the piped edge of the top panel, right sides together. Stitch with the zipper foot as close to the cord as possible, following the piping basting line. Clip corners. Press the seam away from the top panel.
Fit the zip and base panel

Insert a zip into the back gusset section) the zip should be 75 mm shorter than the back length. Fold the gusset back edge to the finished depth, stitch the zip on both sides, and machine the gusset ends closed. Sew the base panel to the lower edge of the gusset, right sides together, leaving the back open at the zip section. Turn through the zip. Push the foam in through the zip opening.
